finding.summary: The track has a high true peak level of 0.8 dBFS, close to 0 dBFS, while the integrated loudness is relatively low at -20 LUFS, indicating that peaky transients are driving the level up without much body.
finding.probable_cause: The recording was captured too hot with insufficient headroom, leaving no safety margin for inter-sample peaks. The absence of any dynamics processing in the FX chain means the raw transients are preserved.
finding.confidence_reason: The true peak measurement is concrete, and the silence fraction is low, so the readings are reliable. However, only three 1/3‑octave spectrum bands are provided, limiting tonal assessment.

evidence_refs.description: True peak of 0.8 dBFS – only 0.8 dB below 0 dBFS, leaving minimal headroom before inter-sample clipping.
Crest factor of 12 dB – indicates moderate peak-to-average ratio, but the peaks are still too close to 0 dBFS.
Three-band spectrum shows a gentle roll-off from 100 Hz (-18 dB) to 4 kHz (-30 dB); no sign of HF emphasis that would exaggerate true peak, but the limited bands cannot rule out ultrasonic energy.

proposal.reason: Lowering the track volume will reduce the true peak to a safer level, preventing possible inter-sample clipping when the signal is further processed, sent to a bus, or converted to analog. This is a simple gain adjustment with no side effects on tone.
